---
title: "Hospital contacts (LPR)"
subtitle: "LPR2 and LPR3 combined — diagnoses and ICD codes"
date: last-modified
---

The National Patient Register (LPR) is the source of diagnoses and hospital contacts.
It covers all public hospital admissions and outpatient contacts in Denmark.

You use the same extraction pattern as in [Phase 5](05_universelle-moenster.qmd) and [Phase 6](06_foerste-udtraek.qmd) — but LPR is more complex than most registers, because it changed format in 2019 and is split into somatic and psychiatric tables. This page shows how to handle that. It is the most important and probably the most complex part of the guide.

::: {.callout-note}
**This page and Phase 10 are circularly dependent — this is deliberate.**
To build your cohort (Phase 10) you need to know how to extract diagnosis codes and procedures from LPR. To extract diagnosis codes from LPR (this page) you need a cohort. We resolve it as follows: **Phase 9 teaches you the extraction pattern** — we assume you already have a cohort. **[Phase 10](10_byg-din-kohorte.qmd) shows you how to build that cohort** using exactly the pattern you just learned. Read the phases in order, and come back to the Phase 9 code when your cohort is ready.
:::

::: {.callout-note}
The code examples use the column names DST's parquet registers typically have. Your columns may be named differently — check with `names(your_data)` or look them up in [Phase 14 — Register reference](14d_register_reference.qmd).
:::

---

## LPR is split into two periods

In March 2019 LPR changed format. Studies covering the period across 2019 must query **both systems** and combine them.

| | LPR2 somatic | LPR2 psychiatric | LPR3 |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Period** | up to March 2019 | up to March 2019 | March 2019 and onwards |
| **Contact register** | `lpr_adm` | `t_psyk_adm` | `lpr_a_kontakt` |
| **Diagnosis register** | `lpr_diag` | `t_psyk_diag` | `lpr_a_diagnose` |
| **Covers psychiatry** | No | Yes | Yes (both combined) |
| **Join key** | `recnum` | `k_recnum` / `v_recnum`¹ | `dw_ek_kontakt` |
| **Date column** | `d_inddto` (Date) | `d_inddto` (Date) | `kont_starttidspunkt` (datetime)² |
| **pnr column** | `pnr` | `v_cpr`³ | `pnr` |
| **Diagnosis code** | `c_diag` | `c_diag` | `diag_kode` |
| **Diagnosis type** | `c_diagtype` | `c_diagtype` | `diag_kode_type` |
| **Contact type** | `c_pattype` (`"0"` = inpatient) | `c_pattype` | `kont_type` (`"ALCA00"` = inpatient) |

¹ `t_psyk_adm` has `k_recnum`; `t_psyk_diag` has `v_recnum` — rename both to `recnum` before joining.
² datetime format — convert with `as.Date()`.
³ Rename: `rename(pnr = v_cpr)`.

::: {.callout-note}
**Psychiatry: separate in LPR2, combined in LPR3**
Before 2019, psychiatric diagnoses (F-codes: dementia, depression etc.) were stored in separate registers (`t_psyk_adm`, `t_psyk_diag`). The structure resembles somatic LPR2, but column names differ — see the table footnotes above. From March 2019, LPR3 combines both: somatic and psychiatric contacts and diagnoses are in the same tables, and no separate psychiatric query is needed.
:::

```{mermaid}
gantt
    title LPR registers
    dateFormat  YYYY
    axisFormat  %Y
    section LPR2 somatic
    lpr_adm + lpr_diag               :done,   1977, 2019
    section LPR2 psychiatric
    t_psyk_adm + t_psyk_diag         :done,   1995, 2019
    section LPR3 combined
    lpr_a_kontakt + lpr_a_diagnose   :active, 2019, 2027
```

---

## ICD codes and the D-prefix

**ICD-10** (International Classification of Diseases, 10th revision) is the WHO's international system for classifying diseases and conditions. All hospital diagnoses in Denmark are coded with ICD-10, e.g. `G30` for Alzheimer's disease and `F00` for dementia in Alzheimer's.

All ICD-10 codes in DST have a prepended `"D"`:
`"DG30"` (Alzheimer's), `"DF00"` (dementia), `"DI21"` (acute myocardial infarction).

**Never compare with the D-prefix.** Always strip it before comparison:

```r
mutate(icd3 = substr(c_diag, 2, 4))   # "DG30" → "G30" (3-digit code)
mutate(icd4 = substr(c_diag, 2, 5))   # "DI219" → "I219" (4-digit code)
```

`substr(x, start, stop)` keeps characters from position `start` up to and including `stop` (counted from 1). `substr(c_diag, 2, 4)` skips position 1 (the D-prefix) and keeps characters 2, 3 and 4: `"DG30"` → `"G30"`. Use 2–5 for 4-digit codes: `"DI219"` → `"I219"`.

---

## Diagnosis types: A, B and G

| Code | Meaning | When to include |
|---|---|---|
| A | Action diagnosis — primary reason for the contact | Always for outcomes |
| B | Secondary diagnosis — additional condition present | Always for outcomes |
| G | Underlying condition — background comorbidity | Only for baseline comorbidity |

```r
# For outcomes and exclusion diagnoses:
filter(c_diagtype %in% c("A", "B"))

# For baseline comorbidity (NMI, Charlson):
filter(c_diagtype %in% c("A", "B", "G"))
```

---

## The psychiatric LPR2 register

Psychiatric diagnoses (e.g. dementia F00–F03) recorded **before March 2019** are stored in separate registers.
If you forget them, you miss all dementia diagnoses from geriatric psychiatry wards and memory clinics.

```r
psyk_adm  <- open_dataset("E:/workdata/[projectnumber]/cleaned-data/parquet-registers/t_psyk_adm/")  %>%
  rename_with(tolower) %>%
  rename(pnr = v_cpr, recnum = k_recnum)    # column names differ from somatic LPR2: v_cpr → pnr, k_recnum → recnum
psyk_diag <- open_dataset("E:/workdata/[projectnumber]/cleaned-data/parquet-registers/t_psyk_diag/") %>%
  rename_with(tolower) %>%
  rename(recnum = v_recnum)                 # v_recnum → recnum: makes join key consistent with psyk_adm
```

From March 2019, LPR3 covers both — no separate psychiatric query needed.

---

## `senere_afkraeftet` in LPR3

LPR3 flags diagnoses that have been retracted. The standard filter:

```r
filter(is.na(senere_afkraeftet) | senere_afkraeftet != "Ja")
```

The `is.na()` part is deliberate. R's default behaviour is: `NA != "Ja"` returns `NA` — not `TRUE`. A filter treats `NA` as `FALSE` and *drops* the row. `filter(senere_afkraeftet != "Ja")` *alone* would therefore remove all diagnoses that have no retraction marker at all (i.e. `NA` fields) — even though they are definitely not retracted. `is.na(...)` fixes this: "keep the row if the field is `NA` OR if it is not `"Ja"`". The filter thus retains uncategorised diagnoses, which is the safest assumption.

---

## Two-phase strategy for diagnosis extraction

If you have one outcome, you can go straight to filtering. But if you have **two or more outcomes** — e.g. dementia, heart disease and kidney disease — the two-phase strategy is far more efficient and less error-prone.

### Phase 1 — build one outcome-agnostic diagnosis table

Query LPR2 and LPR3 **once** and build a combined table with *all* diagnoses for your cohort members — without filtering on specific ICD codes yet.

The result is `alle_dx`: one large table with one row per diagnosis per person, D-prefix stripped. The columns `pnr`, `date_contact` and `icd3` are all you need for all subsequent outcomes.

**Why?** LPR is very large. Querying it repeatedly — once per outcome — is slow and increases the risk of errors: a silent copy-paste error in one extraction is hard to detect because the code still runs. Build `alle_dx` once and reuse it.

### Phase 2 — filter for the specific outcome

For each outcome:

1. Define `CODES <- c(...)` with the relevant ICD codes
2. Filter `alle_dx` on the codes and on `date_contact > index_date` (only events after study entry)
3. Find **first** occurrence per person: `group_by(pnr) %>% arrange(date_contact) %>% slice(1)`
4. Join to `cohort_clean` with `left_join()` — `NA` in `event_date` = no event = person is censored at end of study

**The only line you change between outcomes is `CODES <- c(...)`.**

If you want to extract **baseline comorbidity** instead (diagnoses *before* study entry), use `filter(date_contact < index_date)` in step 2 — the rest is identical.

---

## Complete pattern: fetch all diagnoses for your cohort

This is Phase 1 — the code to build `alle_dx`.

::: {.callout-note}
The example requires registers available as parquet files and uses `open_dataset()` with generic paths — adjust paths to your project. If your registers are still only in SAS format, convert them first: [Phase 4 — SAS to parquet](04_filformater.qmd#convert-sas-to-parquet). To practise the code locally with synthetic data, see the **Try it yourself** fold-out below.
:::

::: {.callout-note}
**This example assumes a completed cohort.**
`kohort` is the data.frame with `pnr` and `index_date` per person, which you built in [Phase 10 — Build your cohort](10_byg-din-kohorte.qmd). `unique(kohort$pnr)` extracts all pnr's as a vector to filter LPR.

**Practising LPR patterns without a real cohort yet?** Use a test cohort from [Phase 6 — First extraction](06_foerste-udtraek.qmd) (200 random pnr's from BEF), or walk through the code without running it. This page teaches the LPR extraction pattern — cohort construction is Phase 10.
:::

```r
library(arrow)         # open_dataset() — lazy connection to parquet
library(dplyr)         # filter, select, mutate, inner_join, bind_rows

cohort_pnrs <- unique(kohort$pnr)   # vector of all unique pnr's in your cohort — see note above

# ── LPR2 somatic (up to March 2019) ─────────────────────────────────────
lpr_adm  <- open_dataset("E:/workdata/[projectnumber]/cleaned-data/parquet-registers/lpr_adm/")  %>% rename_with(tolower)   # LPR2 contact table — lazy connection
lpr_diag <- open_dataset("E:/workdata/[projectnumber]/cleaned-data/parquet-registers/lpr_diag/") %>% rename_with(tolower)   # LPR2 diagnosis table — lazy connection

lpr2_dx <- lpr_adm %>%
  filter(pnr %in% !!cohort_pnrs) %>%               # only the cohort's pnr's
  select(pnr, recnum, date_contact = d_inddto) %>%   # recnum is the join key to lpr_diag
  inner_join(
    lpr_diag %>%
      filter(c_diagtype %in% c("A", "B")) %>%    # only action and secondary diagnoses
      select(recnum, c_diag),                    # only join key and diagnosis code
    by = "recnum"                                # join on contact number
  ) %>%
  collect() %>%                                  # fetch into R
  mutate(icd3 = substr(c_diag, 2, 4))           # strip D-prefix: "DG30" → "G30"

# ── LPR3 (March 2019 and onwards) ────────────────────────────────────────
lpr3_k <- open_dataset("E:/workdata/[projectnumber]/cleaned-data/parquet-registers/lpr_a_kontakt/")  %>% rename_with(tolower)   # LPR3 contact table — lazy connection
lpr3_d <- open_dataset("E:/workdata/[projectnumber]/cleaned-data/parquet-registers/lpr_a_diagnose/") %>% rename_with(tolower)   # LPR3 diagnosis table — lazy connection

lpr3_dx <- lpr3_k %>%
  filter(pnr %in% !!cohort_pnrs) %>%                              # only the cohort's pnr's
  select(pnr, dw_ek_kontakt, date_contact = kont_starttidspunkt) %>%   # dw_ek_kontakt is the join key to lpr_a_diagnose
  inner_join(
    lpr3_d %>%
      filter(diag_kode_type %in% c("A", "B"),
             is.na(senere_afkraeftet) | senere_afkraeftet != "Ja") %>%   # exclude retracted diagnoses
      select(dw_ek_kontakt, c_diag = diag_kode),                         # rename to c_diag for consistency with LPR2
    by = "dw_ek_kontakt"                                                  # join on LPR3 key
  ) %>%
  collect() %>%                                                  # fetch into R
  mutate(
    date_contact = as.Date(date_contact),  # datetime → date
    icd3 = substr(c_diag, 2, 4)           # strip D-prefix: "DG30" → "G30"
  )

# ── Combine ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
alle_dx <- bind_rows(lpr2_dx, lpr3_dx)   # one diagnosis table covering both periods
```

---

## Exclude prevalent cases

Before extracting new events, you must exclude persons who already had the diagnosis *before* index date — otherwise they count as new cases even though they are not.

```r
EXCL_CODES <- c("G30", "F00", "F01", "F02", "F03")   # define a new R variable with your exclusion codes — change to your own; usually the same as your outcome

# Find all with the diagnosis BEFORE index date
prevalent <- alle_dx %>%
  filter(icd3 %in% EXCL_CODES) %>%                                    # keep only these codes
  inner_join(kohort %>% select(pnr, index_date), by = "pnr") %>%       # add index date
  filter(date_contact < index_date) %>%                                 # ONLY pre-index diagnoses
  distinct(pnr)                                                         # one row per person

# Exclude from cohort — done BEFORE outcome extraction
cat("Cohort before exclusion:  ", nrow(kohort),                       "\n")
cohort_clean <- kohort %>%
  anti_join(prevalent, by = "pnr")                                    # remove prevalent cases
cat("Cohort after exclusion:   ", nrow(cohort_clean),                 "\n")
cat("Excluded:                 ", nrow(kohort) - nrow(cohort_clean),  "\n")
```

::: {.callout-tip}
**Lookback period:** Consider restricting `date_contact < index_date` further — e.g. to only the 5 years before index — to avoid false exclusions based on very old diagnoses. This depends on your definition of "prevalent".

With a 5-year lookback (replace `365*5` with your desired period):

```r
filter(date_contact >= index_date - 365*5,   # earliest boundary: 5 years before index
       date_contact < index_date)             # ONLY pre-index diagnoses
```
:::

---

## Extract a specific outcome (Phase 2)

Now that the cohort is cleaned of prevalent cases, you can extract new events.
Change `CODES` to the ICD codes you want — use `cohort_clean` instead of `kohort`.

```r
CODES <- c("G30", "F00", "F01", "F02", "F03")   # dementia — change to your outcome

outcome <- alle_dx %>%
  filter(icd3 %in% CODES) %>%                                                # keep only the desired codes
  inner_join(cohort_clean %>% select(pnr, index_date), by = "pnr") %>%       # use the cleaned cohort
  filter(date_contact > index_date) %>%   # post-index; use < for baseline covariate
  group_by(pnr) %>%                                                           # group to find first event
  arrange(date_contact) %>%                                                   # oldest date first
  slice(1) %>%                            # first occurrence per person
  ungroup() %>%                                                               # release grouping
  select(pnr, event_date = date_contact)                                      # rename to event_date

# Join to cohort — NA = no event (still at risk, censored at end of study)
result <- cohort_clean %>%
  select(pnr) %>%                         # keep only pnr from cleaned cohort
  left_join(outcome, by = "pnr")           # attach event_date — NA = censored

saveRDS(result, "datasets/extract_dementia.rds")   # change filename for each new outcome
```

---

<details>
<summary>Add psychiatric LPR2 (necessary for F-codes, e.g. dementia)</summary>

```r
psyk_adm  <- open_dataset("E:/workdata/[projectnumber]/cleaned-data/parquet-registers/t_psyk_adm/")  %>%
  rename_with(tolower) %>%
  rename(pnr = v_cpr, recnum = k_recnum)   # v_cpr → pnr; k_recnum → recnum (join key)
psyk_diag <- open_dataset("E:/workdata/[projectnumber]/cleaned-data/parquet-registers/t_psyk_diag/") %>%
  rename_with(tolower) %>%
  rename(recnum = v_recnum)                # v_recnum → recnum: makes join key consistent with psyk_adm

lpr2_psyk_dx <- psyk_adm %>%
  filter(pnr %in% !!cohort_pnrs) %>%               # only the cohort's pnr's
  select(pnr, recnum, date_contact = d_inddto) %>%   # recnum is the join key
  inner_join(
    psyk_diag %>%
      filter(c_diagtype %in% c("A", "B")) %>%    # only action and secondary diagnoses
      select(recnum, c_diag),                    # only join key and diagnosis code
    by = "recnum"                                # join on contact number
  ) %>%
  collect() %>%                                  # fetch into R
  mutate(icd3 = substr(c_diag, 2, 4))           # strip D-prefix: "DF00" → "F00"

alle_dx <- bind_rows(lpr2_dx, lpr2_psyk_dx, lpr3_dx)   # combine all three sources
```

</details>

---

<details>
<summary>Try it yourself — runnable example with synthetic data</summary>

::: {.callout-important}
This example requires **RStudio installed locally on your computer** — not the DST server.
The synthetic dataset (`fakeregs`) is not available on DST.
Download R: [cran.r-project.org](https://cran.r-project.org/) · Download RStudio: [posit.co/download/rstudio-desktop](https://posit.co/download/rstudio-desktop/)
:::

The example extracts CVD diagnoses (ischaemic heart disease, ICD-10 I20–I25) from LPR2 and LPR3 combined — the complete pattern from the theory section above, but runnable locally with synthetic data.

The synthetic LPR data is generated with the `fakeregs` package, which you already know from [Phase 6 — First extraction](06_foerste-udtraek.qmd#preparation-install-fakeregs-and-generate-synthetic-data). If you have already generated and saved data there, `synth_data/lpr_adm/` is ready and you can skip the preparation block.

Adapted from Anders Aasted Isaksen's
[`dev/common_tasks_datatable.qmd`](https://github.com/steno-aarhus/fakeregs/blob/main/dev/common_tasks_datatable.qmd)
in [`fakeregs`](https://github.com/steno-aarhus/fakeregs) (MIT licence, Steno Diabetes Center Aarhus).
Rewritten to dplyr + arrow and adapted to this guide's pattern.

```r
# Install fakeregs for the first time:
# install.packages("pak"); pak::pak("steno-aarhus/fakeregs")

library(fakeregs)   # synthetic DST register data
library(dplyr)      # filter, select, mutate, inner_join, bind_rows
library(arrow)      # open_dataset, write_parquet

# ── Preparation: generate synthetic data and save as parquet (done only once) ────
bp             <- generate_background_pop()
lpr_adm_synth  <- generate_lpr_adm(background_df = bp)
lpr_diag_synth <- generate_lpr_diag(background_df = lpr_adm_synth)
lpr_a_k_synth  <- generate_lpr_a_kontakt(background_df = bp)
lpr_a_d_synth  <- generate_lpr_a_diagnose(background_df = lpr_a_k_synth)

dir.create("synth_data/lpr_adm",        recursive = TRUE, showWarnings = FALSE)
dir.create("synth_data/lpr_diag",       recursive = TRUE, showWarnings = FALSE)
dir.create("synth_data/lpr_a_kontakt",  recursive = TRUE, showWarnings = FALSE)
dir.create("synth_data/lpr_a_diagnose", recursive = TRUE, showWarnings = FALSE)
write_parquet(lpr_adm_synth,  "synth_data/lpr_adm/lpr_adm.parquet")
write_parquet(lpr_diag_synth, "synth_data/lpr_diag/lpr_diag.parquet")
write_parquet(lpr_a_k_synth,  "synth_data/lpr_a_kontakt/lpr_a_kontakt.parquet")
write_parquet(lpr_a_d_synth,  "synth_data/lpr_a_diagnose/lpr_a_diagnose.parquet")
```

::: {.callout-tip}
**The path is relative to your working directory** — check with `getwd()`.
If you have already run the preparation block in [Phase 6](06_foerste-udtraek.qmd), `synth_data/lpr_adm/` is already saved.
:::

```r
# The ICD codes we are looking for — change these to your own outcome
CVD_CODES <- c("I20", "I21", "I22", "I23", "I24", "I25")   # ischaemic heart disease

# ── LPR2 somatic (up to March 2019) ──────────────────────────────────────
lpr_adm  <- open_dataset("synth_data/lpr_adm/")  %>% rename_with(tolower)   # LPR2 contact table — synthetic
lpr_diag <- open_dataset("synth_data/lpr_diag/") %>% rename_with(tolower)   # LPR2 diagnosis table — synthetic

lpr2_cvd <- lpr_adm %>%
  select(pnr, recnum, date_contact = d_inddto) %>%           # select only necessary columns
  inner_join(
    lpr_diag %>%
      filter(c_diagtype %in% c("A", "B"),                    # only action and secondary diagnoses
             substr(c_diag, 2, 4) %in% !!CVD_CODES) %>%       # !! sends the local R vector to DuckDB
      select(recnum, c_diag),                    # only join key and diagnosis code
    by = "recnum"                                             # join key in LPR2
  ) %>%
  collect() %>%                                              # HERE data is fetched into R
  mutate(icd3 = substr(c_diag, 2, 4))                        # save cleaned code as new column

# ── LPR3 (March 2019 and onwards) ─────────────────────────────────────────
lpr3_k <- open_dataset("synth_data/lpr_a_kontakt/")  %>% rename_with(tolower)   # LPR3 contact table — synthetic
lpr3_d <- open_dataset("synth_data/lpr_a_diagnose/") %>% rename_with(tolower)   # LPR3 diagnosis table — synthetic

lpr3_cvd <- lpr3_k %>%
  select(pnr, dw_ek_kontakt, date_contact = kont_starttidspunkt) %>%   # dw_ek_kontakt is join key to lpr_a_diagnose
  inner_join(
    lpr3_d %>%
      filter(diag_kode_type %in% c("A", "B"),
             is.na(senere_afkraeftet) | senere_afkraeftet != "Ja",  # exclude retracted diagnoses
             substr(diag_kode, 2, 4) %in% !!CVD_CODES) %>%   # !! sends the local R vector to DuckDB
      select(dw_ek_kontakt, c_diag = diag_kode),             # rename to c_diag for consistency with LPR2
    by = "dw_ek_kontakt"                                     # join key in LPR3
  ) %>%
  collect() %>%                                              # fetch into R
  mutate(
    date_contact = as.Date(date_contact),                    # datetime → date
    icd3         = substr(c_diag, 2, 4)                      # strip D-prefix: "DI21" → "I21"
  )

# ── Combine and save ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
alle_cvd <- bind_rows(lpr2_cvd, lpr3_cvd)                   # stack LPR2 and LPR3

nrow(alle_cvd)                                               # check: number of diagnosis rows
length(unique(alle_cvd$pnr))                                 # check: number of unique individuals
table(alle_cvd$icd3)                                         # distribution across codes

saveRDS(alle_cvd, "datasets/extract_cvd.rds")                # save — change path to your own folder
```

</details>

---

## Next steps

You have now extracted diagnoses from two LPR generations. Next steps are to shape and combine your extracts:

→ [Phase 11 — Joins and pivots](11_data-manipulation.qmd)

## See also

- [Phase 14 — Register reference](14d_register_reference.qmd) — confirmed column names for all LPR registers
- [Phase 14 — Pitfalls](14c_dst_faldgruber.qmd) — known issues with LPR on DST
- [Phase 6 — First extraction](06_foerste-udtraek.qmd) — step-by-step introduction to open_dataset, collect and saveRDS

### External resources on LPR3 and the 2019 transition

- [ctpteam/DST — "Guide to LPR3"](https://github.com/ctpteam/DST) — institutional guide to the LPR3 structure
- [Aarhus-Psychiatry-Research/diagnostic-stability-lpr2-lpr3](https://github.com/Aarhus-Psychiatry-Research/diagnostic-stability-lpr2-lpr3) — peer-reviewed, thorough example of diagnostic stability across the LPR2→LPR3 transition (methodological inspiration, not code for reuse)
